questions.questionText: The notochord is derived from the:
questions.explanation: The notochord is derived from axial mesoderm. It defines the axis of the embryo, induces the overlying ectoderm to form the neural plate, and forms the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc in adults. Remnants can give rise to chordoma (tumor most common in the sacrococcygeal region and clivus). Reference: Langman's Embryology — Gastrulation.

questions.questionText: The neural crest cells give rise to all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Neural crest cells (ectomesenchyme) give rise to: melanocytes, Schwann cells, adrenal medulla, spinal ganglia (DRG), autonomic ganglia, odontoblasts, C cells of thyroid, aorticopulmonary septum, cranial bones (frontal, parts of skull), pharyngeal arch mesenchyme. Adrenal CORTEX is from intermediate mesoderm. EXAM TRAP: Adrenal medulla = neural crest; cortex = mesoderm. Reference: Langman's — Neural Crest Derivatives.

questions.questionText: Implantation of the blastocyst normally occurs on which day after fertilization?
questions.explanation: Normal implantation occurs on day 6-7 at the upper posterior wall of the uterus. Timeline: fertilization (day 0) → 2-cell (day 1) → morula (day 3-4) → blastocyst (day 4-5) → implantation begins (day 6-7) → complete implantation (day 10-11). Ectopic implantation most commonly occurs in the fallopian tube (ampulla is MC site). Reference: Langman's — Implantation.

questions.questionText: The most common site of ectopic pregnancy is the:
questions.explanation: The ampulla of the fallopian tube is the most common site of ectopic pregnancy (~70%). The ampulla is also the normal site of fertilization. Risk factors: PID, previous ectopic, IUD, tubal surgery, endometriosis. The isthmus is the second most common site. Ovarian and cervical ectopics are rare. Reference: Langman's — Abnormal Implantation.

questions.questionText: The first organ to function in the embryo is the:
questions.explanation: The heart is the first functioning organ in the embryo. It starts beating by day 22 (3rd week). The heart tube forms from splanchnic mesoderm (cardiogenic plate). Heart development: cardiogenic plate → heart tube → cardiac loop (dextral looping — determines left-right asymmetry). Situs inversus occurs with abnormal looping. Reference: Langman's — Cardiovascular Development.

questions.questionText: Teratogenicity is maximum during which period of pregnancy?
questions.explanation: The period of organogenesis (weeks 3-8/embryonic period) is the most critical for teratogenicity. During weeks 1-2, the embryo follows the 'all or none' law (either dies or survives normally). After week 8 (fetal period), organs are mostly formed — teratogens cause growth retardation/functional defects rather than major structural malformations. Reference: Langman's — Teratology.

questions.questionText: The embryonic structure that gives rise to the liver, pancreas, and lungs is the:
questions.explanation: Endoderm gives rise to the epithelial lining of the GI tract, respiratory tract, and associated organs: liver, pancreas (parenchyma), thyroid (follicular cells), parathyroid, thymus, lungs (epithelium), urinary bladder epithelium. The connective tissue and smooth muscle of these organs come from splanchnic mesoderm. Reference: Langman's — Germ Layer Derivatives.

questions.questionText: The pharyngeal (branchial) arch that gives rise to the muscles of facial expression is the:
questions.explanation: Second pharyngeal arch (Reichert's cartilage, supplied by CN VII): gives rise to muscles of facial expression, stapedius, stylohyoid, posterior belly of digastric, stapes, styloid process, lesser cornu of hyoid. First arch (CN V3): muscles of mastication, malleus, incus, Meckel's cartilage. Third arch (CN IX): stylopharyngeus. Fourth-sixth arches (CN X): laryngeal and pharyngeal muscles. Reference: Langman's — Pharyngeal Arches.

questions.questionText: The septum transversum contributes to the formation of the:
questions.explanation: The diaphragm develops from 4 sources: (1) Septum transversum → central tendon (largest contribution), (2) Pleuroperitoneal membranes, (3) Dorsal mesentery of esophagus → crura, (4) Body wall mesoderm → peripheral muscular part. Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (Bochdalek hernia) = failure of pleuroperitoneal membrane closure (left posterolateral, most common). Reference: Langman's — Diaphragm Development.

questions.questionText: Tetralogy of Fallot results from:
questions.optionC: Unequal division of truncus arteriosus (anterior deviation of aorticopulmonary septum)
questions.explanation: TOF results from anterior and superior deviation of the aorticopulmonary (conotruncal) septum. This causes: (1) pulmonary stenosis (infundibular), (2) overriding aorta, (3) VSD (malalignment type), (4) RVH (secondary). TOF is the most common cyanotic CHD. Boot-shaped heart on X-ray. Tet spells (hypercyanotic) treated by knee-chest position. Neural crest cells form the aorticopulmonary septum. Reference: Langman's — Heart Development.

questions.questionText: The first pharyngeal pouch gives rise to:
questions.explanation: Pharyngeal pouches (endoderm): 1st pouch → tubotympanic recess (middle ear cavity, Eustachian tube, mastoid antrum). 2nd pouch → palatine tonsils. 3rd pouch → inferior parathyroid (dorsal wing) + thymus (ventral wing). 4th pouch → superior parathyroid + ultimobranchial body (C cells/parafollicular cells). EXAM TRAP: Inferior parathyroid from 3rd pouch, superior from 4th. Reference: Langman's — Pharyngeal Pouches.

questions.questionText: The mesonephric (Wolffian) duct in males develops into:
questions.explanation: Mesonephric (Wolffian) duct in males (under influence of testosterone from Sertoli cells): epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicle, ejaculatory duct. In females, it regresses (remnants: epoophoron, paroophoron, Gartner's duct). Paramesonephric (Müllerian) duct → female structures: fallopian tubes, uterus, upper 2/3 vagina. AMH (from Sertoli cells) causes Müllerian duct regression in males. Reference: Langman's — Genital System.

questions.questionText: The vitellointestinal duct (yolk stalk) normally obliterates. Its persistence gives rise to:
questions.explanation: The vitellointestinal duct (OMD — omphalomesenteric duct) connects the midgut to the yolk sac. Normally obliterates by week 7. Persistence → Meckel's diverticulum (most common congenital GI anomaly, true diverticulum on anti-mesenteric border of ileum, 2 feet from ICV). Other remnants: vitelline fistula, enterocyst, fibrous band (causing volvulus). Reference: Langman's — Midgut Development.

questions.questionText: The thyroid gland develops from which pharyngeal structure?
questions.optionB: Foramen cecum of the tongue (endoderm of floor of pharynx)
questions.explanation: The thyroid develops from endoderm at the foramen cecum (junction of anterior 2/3 and posterior 1/3 of tongue). It descends in front of the hyoid bone and laryngeal cartilages, connected by the thyroglossal duct (normally obliterates). Persistence → thyroglossal cyst (midline neck swelling that moves with swallowing AND tongue protrusion). Ectopic thyroid: lingual thyroid (most common). Reference: Langman's — Thyroid Development.

questions.questionText: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (Bochdalek hernia) most commonly occurs on the:
questions.explanation: Bochdalek hernia occurs due to failure of closure of the pleuroperitoneal canal (posterolateral defect). Left side in ~85% of cases (right side closes earlier and is protected by the liver). Abdominal viscera herniate into the thorax → pulmonary hypoplasia → respiratory distress at birth. Morgagni hernia = anterior (retrosternal) diaphragmatic hernia, less common. Reference: Langman's — Diaphragm Development.

questions.questionText: During fetal circulation, oxygenated blood from the placenta reaches the fetus via the:
questions.explanation: The single umbilical vein carries oxygenated blood from the placenta to the fetus. It enters the liver → partly through ductus venosus (bypasses liver to IVC) and partly through hepatic sinusoids. Two umbilical arteries carry deoxygenated blood FROM the fetus TO the placenta. Memory: 'Arteries carry AWAY, Vein comes IN' — opposite to postnatal because it's from placenta. Reference: Langman's — Fetal Circulation.

questions.questionText: The ductus arteriosus connects the:
questions.optionC: Right atrium to the left atrium
questions.explanation: Ductus arteriosus connects the pulmonary trunk (left pulmonary artery) to the aortic arch, shunting blood away from the non-functioning fetal lungs. It closes functionally within hours of birth (prostaglandin withdrawal) and structurally within 2-3 months (becoming ligamentum arteriosum). Patent ductus arteriosus: continuous machinery murmur, treated with indomethacin (inhibits prostaglandin synthesis). PGE1 keeps PDA open when needed. Reference: Langman's — Fetal Circulation.

questions.questionText: The midgut undergoes physiological herniation through the umbilicus during which week of development?
questions.explanation: The midgut herniates through the umbilical ring during weeks 6-10 due to rapid growth of the liver and intestines. It undergoes 270° counterclockwise rotation around the SMA axis and returns to the abdomen by week 10. Failure of return → omphalocele (covered by peritoneum/amnion). Gastroschisis = paraumbilical herniation (NOT covered, usually right of umbilicus). Non-rotation/malrotation → Ladd's bands → volvulus. Reference: Langman's — Midgut Development.

questions.questionText: The spinal cord develops from the:
questions.explanation: The neural tube (formed by neurulation — folding of the neural plate, induced by the notochord) gives rise to the entire CNS: brain and spinal cord. Neural tube defects (NTDs): anencephaly (failure of cranial neuropore closure, day 25), spina bifida (failure of caudal neuropore closure, day 27). Prevention: folic acid supplementation (preconceptional). Reference: Langman's — Neurulation.

questions.questionText: The allantois in the human embryo becomes the:
questions.explanation: The allantois extends from the hindgut into the connecting stalk. It partially forms the urinary bladder and becomes the urachus (connecting bladder apex to umbilicus). After birth, it becomes the median umbilical ligament. Patent urachus → urine draining from the umbilicus. Urachal cyst/sinus can develop from incomplete obliteration. Urachal carcinoma (adenocarcinoma) can arise. Reference: Langman's — Urogenital System.

questions.questionText: Tracheoesophageal fistula is most commonly associated with:
questions.explanation: Esophageal atresia with distal TEF (Type C, Gross classification) is the most common type (~85%). The proximal esophagus ends as a blind pouch, and the distal esophagus connects to the trachea. Clinical: polyhydramnios (fetus unable to swallow amniotic fluid), inability to pass NG tube, choking with first feed. VACTERL association: Vertebral, Anal, Cardiac, TEF, Renal, Limb. Reference: Langman's — Respiratory System Development.

questions.questionText: The pronephros, mesonephros, and metanephros develop from:
questions.explanation: All three kidney systems develop from intermediate mesoderm (nephrogenic cord). Pronephros (cervical, non-functional, degenerates by week 4). Mesonephros (thoracolumbar, functions transiently). Metanephros (permanent kidney, from 2 sources: ureteric bud/metanephric diverticulum from mesonephric duct → collecting system, and metanephrogenic blastema → nephrons). Reference: Langman's — Urinary System.

questions.questionText: The testes develop in the abdomen and descend into the scrotum guided by the:
questions.explanation: The gubernaculum testis guides testicular descent from the abdomen through the inguinal canal into the scrotum (begins at 7th month, completes by birth). The processus vaginalis (peritoneal evagination) precedes the testis through the inguinal canal. Patent processus vaginalis → indirect inguinal hernia or communicating hydrocele. Cryptorchidism = failure of descent (risk of infertility and testicular cancer). Reference: Langman's — Testicular Descent.

questions.questionText: The cleft palate results from failure of fusion of:
questions.optionB: Lateral palatine shelves with each other and nasal septum
questions.explanation: The secondary palate forms from the palatine shelves (from maxillary prominences) that grow medially, elevate, and fuse with each other and the nasal septum (weeks 7-12). Failure of fusion → cleft palate. Cleft lip results from failure of fusion of the medial nasal prominence with the maxillary prominence. Isolated cleft palate is more common in females; cleft lip ± palate is more common in males. Reference: Langman's — Face Development.

questions.questionText: The most common type of atrial septal defect is:
questions.explanation: Ostium secundum ASD (in the region of fossa ovalis) is the most common type (~75%). It results from excessive resorption of septum primum or inadequate development of septum secundum. Ostium primum ASD is associated with endocardial cushion defects (Down syndrome). Sinus venosus ASD is associated with anomalous pulmonary venous drainage. Reference: Langman's — Atrial Septation.

questions.questionText: Horseshoe kidney is trapped under which artery during its ascent?
questions.explanation: Horseshoe kidney: lower poles of both kidneys fuse across the midline (at the isthmus). During normal ascent, the fused kidney gets trapped under the inferior mesenteric artery (at L3) and cannot ascend to its normal position (L1). It is the most common renal fusion anomaly. Usually asymptomatic but has increased risk of stones, UTI, and UPJ obstruction. Associated with Turner syndrome. Reference: Langman's — Kidney Development.

questions.questionText: The structure derived from the first pharyngeal arch cartilage (Meckel's cartilage) is:
questions.explanation: First arch (Meckel's cartilage, CN V3): malleus, incus, anterior ligament of malleus, sphenomandibular ligament. Second arch (Reichert's cartilage, CN VII): stapes, styloid process, stylohyoid ligament, lesser cornu of hyoid, upper body of hyoid. Third arch (CN IX): greater cornu and lower body of hyoid. Fourth arch: thyroid cartilage. Reference: Langman's — Pharyngeal Arch Derivatives.

questions.questionText: The trachea and esophagus are separated during development by the:
questions.explanation: The tracheoesophageal septum divides the foregut into ventral (laryngotracheal tube → trachea, bronchi, lungs) and dorsal (esophagus) parts during weeks 4-5. Incomplete separation → tracheoesophageal fistula (TEF). The urorectal septum divides the cloaca into urogenital sinus (ventral) and rectum (dorsal). Aorticopulmonary septum divides the truncus arteriosus. Reference: Langman's — Respiratory Development.
